g.data-narration: Welcome to our exploration of Memory Hierarchy and Cache Memory - essential concepts in computer architecture.
Memory hierarchy organizes memory types in a pyramid, balancing speed, capacity, and cost from fastest to slowest.
Cache bridges the speed gap between CPU and RAM, preventing processor stalls and dramatically improving performance.
g.text: Without cache: CPU waits 200-400 cycles for data from RAM!

g.data-narration: Cache operates on two key principles: temporal locality (reusing recent data) and spatial locality (accessing nearby data).
g.text: Recently accessed data will be accessed again soon
Data near recently accessed location will be accessed

g.data-narration: A cache consists of cache lines storing data, tags identifying memory blocks, and status bits for validation.
Direct mapping assigns each memory block to exactly one cache line using a modulo function, simple but can cause conflicts.
g.text: Each memory block maps to ONE specific cache line using: Line = (Block Address) mod (Total Lines)

g.data-narration: Fully associative mapping allows any block in any line, maximizing flexibility but requiring expensive hardware comparators.
g.text: Any memory block can be placed in ANY cache line - maximum flexibility but expensive hardware

g.data-narration: Set associative mapping divides cache into sets, each with multiple lines, balancing flexibility with practical hardware costs.
g.text: Cache divided into sets; block maps to a set, can go in any line (way) within that set

g.data-narration: Cache performance is measured by hit rate (cache finds data) and miss rate (data not found), determining overall efficiency.
g.text: Cache Performance: Hit Rate and Miss Rate

g.data-narration: Average Memory Access Time combines hit time and miss penalty, showing how cache improves effective memory speed.
g.text: The key metric for cache performance optimization in computer systems
AMAT = Hit Time + (Miss Rate × Miss Penalty)
Hit Time = 1ns | Miss Rate = 0.08 | Miss Penalty = 100ns

g.data-narration: Write policies determine how data modifications are handled: write-through writes to both cache and memory, write-back delays memory write.
g.text: Write to BOTH cache and main memory
✓ Simple | ✓ Consistent | ✗ Slow (always writes to RAM)
Write to cache first; write to RAM only when evicted

g.data-narration: When cache is full, replacement policies decide which line to evict: LRU, FIFO, or Random based on temporal locality.
g.text: When a cache miss occurs and all lines in a set are full, which line to evict?
Evict the block used longest ago
Evict the block that entered first
Simple but may evict useful data

g.data-narration: Let us solve a practical example: calculating cache line index and tag for address 0x1234BC in a 1024-line direct-mapped cache.
